items.subtopic: The Practice of Medicine | ENDURING VALUES OF THE MEDICAL PROFESSION
items.title: Importance of Physician-Patient Relationship in Effective Care
items.content: Despite advancements in medical technology and knowledge, the core value of a trusting physician-patient relationship remains crucial for effective patient care.
keyPoints: The ultimate goal of medicine is to prevent disease and provide early diagnosis and effective treatment when it occurs.
A trusting relationship between physician and patient is central to effective patient care.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · The Practice of Medicine | ENDURING VALUES OF THE MEDICAL PROFESSION

items.subtopic: The Practice of Medicine | ENDURING VALUES OF THE MEDICAL PROFESSION
items.title: Integration of Scientific Knowledge with Human Understanding in Medical Practice
items.content: While scientific advancements have transformed medical practice, physicians must balance technical skills and scientific knowledge with human understanding to care for patients effectively.
keyPoints: Physicians need technical skill, scientific knowledge, and human understanding in patient care.
Patients are seen as humans who seek relief, help, and reassurance beyond their symptoms.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · The Practice of Medicine | ENDURING VALUES OF THE MEDICAL PROFESSION

items.subtopic: THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE
items.title: Integration of Advanced Technologies in Clinical Practice
items.content: The integration of advanced technologies such as biochemistry, genomics, and imaging techniques enhances the understanding and treatment of diseases by providing deeper insights into cellular mechanisms and disease etiology.
keyPoints: Advances in biochemistry, cell biology, immunology, and genomics provide a window into the body's innermost parts.
Newly developed imaging techniques allow access to remote recesses of the body.
Understanding of genes and single cells aids in unraveling disease causes.
Stem cells play roles in tissue renewal, cancer development, and treatment.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE

items.subtopic: THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE
items.title: The Role of Artificial Intelligence in Modern Medicine
items.content: Artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly expanding in the medical field, offering potential for enhanced clinical predictive accuracy through unbiased analysis of large data sets. However, understanding its optimal use and limitations is crucial.
keyPoints: AI is expanding rapidly in medicine.
Machine learning enhances clinical predictive accuracy with big data from omic analyses and electronic records.
It's essential to understand AI's value and limitations without fully relying on it for decision-making.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE

items.subtopic: THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE
items.title: Balancing Technology with Clinical Judgment in Medicine
items.content: Despite technological advances, effective clinical practice requires physicians to skillfully interpret complex histories, physical examinations, and test results, distinguishing relevant findings from incidental ones.
keyPoints: Physicians must identify crucial elements in a patient's history and examination.
Appropriate laboratory and imaging tests are essential for diagnosis.
Clinicians need to discern whether findings warrant further investigation or dismissal as 'red herrings'.
Judgment is key in weighing the risks of tests, preventive measures, or treatments against the disease.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE

items.title: The Importance of Patient History in Diagnosis
items.content: A comprehensive patient history is crucial for forming a differential diagnosis and understanding the patient's perspective on their illness. It involves listening to the patient's narrative, reviewing systems methodically, and considering social and family histories.
keyPoints: All facts of medical significance should be included in the recorded history.
Patients' descriptions and body language can provide valuable insights into their symptoms.
The reported medical history should be corroborated whenever possible.
A thorough family history may require input from multiple relatives.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 1)

items.title: Establishing a Strong Patient-Physician Relationship
items.content: Effective communication and empathy during the history-taking process are essential for building trust, which encourages patients to share more complete information. This relationship is foundational for understanding the patient's view of their illness and expectations.
keyPoints: Patients who feel comfortable with the physician will offer more complete information.
The confidentiality of the patient-physician relationship cannot be overemphasized.
A hurried approach may lead patients to withhold relevant information.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 1)

items.title: Conducting a Methodical Physical Examination
items.content: A thorough physical examination, extending from head to toe, is vital for identifying signs of disease that confirm or suggest new diagnoses. This process should be methodical and considerate of the patient's comfort.
keyPoints: The physical examination should identify objective indications of disease.
Physical examination skills are enhanced by direct observation and feedback from experienced clinicians.
The significance of physical signs is enhanced when they confirm changes suggested by the patient's history.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 1)

items.title: The Importance of Physical Examination Despite Advanced Diagnostics
items.content: Despite the availability of highly sensitive diagnostic tests, a thorough physical examination remains crucial. It can identify clinical findings that may be missed by diagnostics and render certain laboratory results unimportant.
keyPoints: A hands-on examination fosters patient-physician communication and reassurance.
Physical exams can make some lab findings irrelevant (e.g., echocardiographic regurgitant lesions).
Physicians should not rely solely on diagnostic tests due to their limitations.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 2)

items.title: Balancing Diagnostic Tests with Clinical Observation
items.content: While laboratory and imaging tests are essential, physicians must balance the use of these tools with careful clinical observation. Understanding test limitations is crucial to avoid over-reliance on their apparent precision.
keyPoints: Diagnostic tests have inherent fallibility in instruments and interpretation.
Physicians should weigh the cost against the value of information provided by tests.
Tests do not replace the need for thorough patient observation.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 2)

items.title: Judicious Use of Imaging Studies in Medical Decision-Making
items.content: Advanced imaging studies offer detailed anatomic information but should be used judiciously. Physicians must consider whether the results will alter management and benefit the patient.
keyPoints: Imaging tests like MRI, CT, ultrasonography, isotopic scans, and PET provide detailed anatomic data.
Unexpected findings from imaging can lead to unnecessary costs or irrelevant diagnoses.
Physicians should ensure that imaging studies are likely to change management and benefit the patient.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 2)

items.title: Importance of Clear Communication in Team-Based Care
items.content: Effective communication is crucial in team-based care, especially when managing complex medical situations. Physicians should meet face-to-face or by phone to ensure clear planning and consistent messaging to patients.
keyPoints: Face-to-face or phone meetings between physicians are essential for clear communication.
Consistency in messages from various care providers is important for patient understanding.
Effective communication is the most challenging aspect of team-based care.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 1)

items.title: Role Clarity and Introduction in Team-Based Care
items.content: In a hospital setting, patients encounter various health professionals. It is important for providers to introduce themselves, explain their roles, and wear identifying information to reduce patient confusion.
keyPoints: Patients may interact with multiple allied health professionals during hospitalization.
Providers should introduce themselves and explain their roles to the patient.
Wearing nametags or other identifying information helps clarify roles.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 1)

items.title: Transition Between Inpatient and Outpatient Care
items.content: The transition between inpatient and outpatient care involves multiple handoffs, which can complicate communication. Ensuring continuity of information flow is vital for patient management.
keyPoints: Handoffs occur during transitions such as from outpatient to inpatient settings or vice versa.
Continuity of communication between primary care physicians and hospital staff is crucial.
Emerging 'home hospital' settings require adequate home support for select patients.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 1)

items.title: The Role of Primary Care Physicians in Coordinating Specialist Care
items.content: Primary care physicians must coordinate the involvement of multiple healthcare providers to ensure cohesive patient guidance through illness. They should explain specialists' roles and assure patients that decisions are made collaboratively.
keyPoints: Primary care physicians coordinate specialist collaborations.
They provide reassurance about specialist involvement in decision-making.
The 'medical home' concept integrates team-based primary care with subspecialty care.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 2)

items.title: Mitigating Stress During Acute Illness
items.content: Physicians can reduce patient stress during acute illness by providing clear, calm, and consistent information. Recognizing the intimidating hospital environment helps in guiding patients through their experience.
keyPoints: Acute illnesses cause significant stress due to unfamiliar environments.
Reassurance and repeated information help mitigate stress.
Home-hospital care improves patient experience by reducing conventional hospitalization stress.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 2)

items.title: Principles of Medical Decision-Making
items.content: Medical decision-making involves ordering tests, consultations, and treatments based on an understanding of disease pathophysiology. It requires evidence-based approaches, contrasting with anecdotal experiences.
keyPoints: Decision-making is iterative, adjusting as new information arises.
Evidence-based medicine relies on clinical trial data for decisions.
Many decisions are based on expert consensus due to limited rigorous evidence.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 2)

items.title: The Role of Clinical Judgment in Medical Decision-Making
items.content: Medical decision-making often relies on clinical judgment, especially when evidence is lacking. Physicians synthesize known factors and uncertainties using tools like diagnostic tests, Bayes' theorem, and multivariate models to make informed decisions.
keyPoints: Clinical judgment is crucial in the absence of a robust evidence base.
Diagnostic tests complement history and physical examination by reducing uncertainty.
Sensitivity (true-positive rate) and specificity (true-negative rate) are key metrics for test accuracy.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 3)

items.title: Utilization of Practice Guidelines in Patient Care
items.content: Practice guidelines developed by professional organizations help ensure evidence-based, cost-effective care. They aid decision-making and protect against substandard care or malpractice claims but must be applied judiciously due to their inherent limitations.
keyPoints: Guidelines provide a framework for managing specific diagnoses or symptoms.
They can prevent overuse of medical resources and protect patients with inadequate benefits.
Physicians should integrate guidelines without being constrained by them, considering individual patient uniqueness.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 3)

items.title: Precision Medicine: Tailoring Treatment to Individual Patients
items.content: Precision medicine involves stratifying diseases based on genetic and other characteristics to tailor therapies. It aims to improve outcomes by targeting treatments more specifically, minimizing side effects for non-responders.
keyPoints: Diseases are further stratified using genetic, biomarker, phenotypic, and psychosocial data.
Precision medicine refines clinical practice guidelines for subgroups within diagnoses.
Genetic testing in cancers predicts responses to targeted therapies; FDA certifies test validity based on clinical trial data.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 3)

items.title: Importance of Risk-Adjusted Outcome Measures
items.content: Outcome measures such as mortality, length of hospital stay, or readmission rates are typically risk-adjusted to account for the complexity of a patient's clinical condition. This adjustment is crucial because patients often present with multiple complicating background illnesses.
keyPoints: Objective and subjective outcomes both matter in evaluating therapeutic interventions.
Outcome measures like mortality, length of hospital stay, or readmission rates are risk-adjusted.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 4)

items.title: Reducing Medical Errors through Systematic Changes
items.content: Health care systems must implement fundamental changes to reduce medical error rates and improve patient safety. This includes using electronic ordering systems, enforcing hand-washing protocols, and adhering to standardized procedures for invasive devices.
keyPoints: Institute of Medicine reports emphasize reducing medical errors by redesigning health care systems.
Medication errors can be reduced with electronic processes or clear handwriting.
Standardized protocols reduce central-line infections and catheter-associated urinary tract infections.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 4)

items.title: Role of Electronic Medical Records in Enhancing Patient Safety
items.content: Electronic Medical Records (EMRs) play a crucial role in reducing medical errors by providing rapid access to comprehensive patient data. They support real-time monitoring and reduce unnecessary variations in care.
keyPoints: EMRs enhance health care quality and patient safety.
They provide access to historical, clinical information, imaging studies, lab results, and medication records.
Technological limitations and privacy concerns can limit EMR use.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 4)

items.title: Balancing EMR Use with Patient Interaction
items.content: Electronic Medical Records (EMRs) offer significant advantages such as easy access to medical history and data. However, they can create a barrier between physicians and patients if not managed properly. Physicians must ensure face-to-face contact is preserved to maintain the quality of patient care.
keyPoints: EMRs require training and time for data entry.
Providers may feel pressured to take shortcuts like 'cutting and pasting' notes, increasing error risk.
The structured format of EMRs can disrupt traditional narrative flow.
Despite frustrations, EMRs provide ready access to comprehensive patient information.
Physician dissatisfaction with EMR use impacts professional and personal well-being.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 5)

items.title: The Role of Clinical Judgment in the Age of Information Technology
items.content: While EMRs are valuable tools for accessing patient data, they cannot replace the clinical judgment and decision-making skills of physicians. The integration of clinical knowledge with quantitative tools remains essential for optimal medical practice.
keyPoints: Information technology is a tool that supports but does not replace physician decision-making.
Clinical decisions should be based on a combination of clinical knowledge and patient needs.
Quantitative tools supplement, but do not substitute, the nuanced understanding required in medicine.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 5)

items.title: The Importance of Physician-Patient Relationship
items.content: A strong personal relationship between physician and patient is crucial for effective diagnosis and treatment. Physicians must approach patients as individuals, not merely as cases or diseases, fostering a professional attitude with warmth and openness to alleviate anxiety.
keyPoints: Diagnosis and treatment are directly dependent on the patient-physician relationship.
Physicians should avoid being arrogant, patronizing, impatient, or hurried.
Empathy and compassion are essential for caring physicians.
Consideration of familial, social, and cultural backgrounds is important.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 1)

items.title: Principles of Informed Consent
items.content: Informed consent involves clear communication about treatment options, risks, benefits, and consequences. Physicians must ensure patients understand these aspects to respect their autonomy and act in the patient's best interest.
keyPoints: Physicians must communicate alternatives for care clearly.
Encouraging questions is crucial for understanding.
Repeated discussions may be necessary to enhance patient comprehension.
Physicians should avoid conflicts of interest when obtaining consent.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 1)

items.title: Approach to Grave Prognoses and Death
items.content: Transparency in communication is essential when dealing with terminal illnesses. Physicians should assess the patient's desire for information and involve them in decision-making, considering their religious beliefs and emotional needs.
keyPoints: Information about incurable diseases must be delivered transparently.
Patients often have insights into their condition despite family protection.
Assessing the appropriate time and pace for sharing information is crucial.
Patient participation in decision-making should consider palliation goals.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 1)

items.title: Importance of Holistic Care in Terminal Illness
items.content: In terminal illness, the physician's role shifts from cure to comprehensive care. This involves addressing emotional, physical, and spiritual needs while ensuring dignity and minimizing isolation.
keyPoints: Primum succurrere: first to help is a guiding principle.
Physicians should provide information to family members and manage their grief, guilt, or anger.
Maintain human dignity and avoid patient isolation from family and friends.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 2)

items.title: Challenges in Modern Healthcare Delivery
items.content: Modern healthcare trends can make care feel impersonal. Physicians must strive to maintain humanism and professionalism despite these challenges.
keyPoints: Trends include cost reduction efforts, managed-care programs, technological reliance, team-based care, and telemedicine.
The Charter on Medical Professionalism emphasizes patient welfare, autonomy, and social justice.
Physicians should exhibit availability, concern, explanation willingness, and a nonjudgmental attitude.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 2)

items.title: Prioritizing Patient Quality of Life
items.content: Understanding and prioritizing the patient's quality of life is crucial, requiring detailed knowledge through candid conversations.
keyPoints: Quality of life involves subjective assessment of what each patient values most.
Detailed knowledge often requires unhurried, repeated conversations with the patient.
Patient encounters should focus on the patient rather than distractions like EMR data.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 2)

items.title: The Role of Genomics in Medicine
items.content: Genomics has revolutionized the diagnosis, prevention, and treatment of diseases by providing insights into genetic and environmental interactions. The sequencing of the human genome marked the beginning of this era, leading to rapid advancements like the swift development of vaccines for COVID-19.
keyPoints: Complete sequencing of the human genome was announced in 2003.
Genomics has facilitated the development of gene expression profiles and genotyping.
Rapid sequencing of pathogens (e.g., H1N1, COVID-19) aids vaccine development.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)

items.title: Epigenetics in Disease
items.content: Epigenetic changes, influenced by environmental factors, affect gene expression without altering DNA sequences. These alterations are linked to various cancers and diseases, highlighting the importance of epigenetic regulation in diverse phenotypes.
keyPoints: Epigenetics involves chromatin, histone modifications, and DNA methylation.
Identical DNA can result in diverse phenotypes due to epigenetic regulation.
Epigenetic alterations are associated with cancers and other diseases.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)

items.title: Microbiomics and Health
items.content: The human microbiome, consisting of over 3–4 million microbial genes, plays a crucial role in immune system maturation, metabolic balance, brain function, and disease susceptibility. Environmental factors like antibiotic use can significantly impact these microbial communities.
keyPoints: Human haploid genome has ~23,000 genes; microbiome contains >3–4 million genes.
Microbiomes influence immune, metabolic, and neurological health.
Antibiotic overuse is linked to increased obesity, atherosclerosis, and immune diseases.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)

items.title: The Role of Continuous Learning in Omics
items.content: Physicians must engage in continuous learning to judiciously apply rapidly evolving omic technologies and information for patient benefit.
keyPoints: Omics are evolving rapidly, requiring continuous physician education.
Genetic testing necessitates understanding its value, limitations, and implications.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)

items.title: Globalization's Impact on Medicine
items.content: Physicians should be aware of global disease spread due to travel, migration, climate change, and telemedicine.
keyPoints: Global travel affects disease profiles; example: Zika virus in the Americas.
Telemedicine facilitates international consultation and skill transfer.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)

items.title: Internet's Influence on Medicine
items.content: The Internet provides vast medical information but requires discernment due to potential misinformation.
keyPoints: The Internet offers instant access to medical resources and patient education.
Physicians must navigate misinformation risks, especially from social media.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)

items.title: Impact of Pay-for-Performance on Healthcare
items.content: The pay-for-performance movement links reimbursement to the quality of care, aiming to improve healthcare standards and control costs. This shift emphasizes preventive strategies and requires physicians to justify hospitalizations based on illness complexity.
keyPoints: Reimbursement is increasingly tied to documented nature and complexity of illness.
Physicians must defend hospitalization duration if it deviates from 'average' standards.
Managed care contracts emphasize cost management and preventive care.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 3)

items.title: Ethical Considerations in Emerging Medical Technologies
items.content: Physicians must navigate ethical issues related to new technologies like gene editing and nanotechnology, which can alter human performance and disease predispositions. Shared decision-making is crucial in areas such as genetic counseling.
keyPoints: Technological advances extend beyond traditional medical goals.
Ethical debates are essential for appropriate use of new interventions.
Shared decision-making is vital in diagnostic and treatment options.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 3)

items.title: Challenges in Physician Training and Effectiveness
items.content: Restrictions on training hours aim to reduce fatigue-related errors, but increase patient handoffs. Ensuring thorough transitions of responsibility is critical for maintaining care quality.
keyPoints: ACGME introduced strict training parameters to enhance learning and reduce errors.
Fewer bedside hours lead to more frequent patient handoffs.
Careful handling of information exchange during handoffs is imperative.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 3)

items.subtopic: The Practice of Medicine | ENDURING VALUES OF THE MEDICAL PROFESSION
items.question: According to Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, what is considered the ultimate goal of medicine?
options: To prevent disease and provide effective treatment when it occurs.
To develop new imaging techniques and bioinformatics tools.
To ensure physicians have access to electronic medical records.
To increase the scientific knowledge available in the field.
items.explanation: The ultimate goal of medicine is stated as preventing disease, diagnosing it early when it occurs, and providing effective treatment.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · The Practice of Medicine | ENDURING VALUES OF THE MEDICAL PROFESSION

items.subtopic: The Practice of Medicine | ENDURING VALUES OF THE MEDICAL PROFESSION
items.question: What has fundamentally changed the way physicians define, diagnose, treat, and attempt to prevent disease since 1950?
options: The increase in human understanding and sympathy.
Advances in molecular genetics, imaging techniques, robotics, bioinformatics, and information technology.
The establishment of electronic medical records.
The growing number of physicians entering the field.
items.explanation: Scientific advances such as molecular genetics, new imaging techniques, robotics, and bioinformatics have fundamentally changed medical practice.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · The Practice of Medicine | ENDURING VALUES OF THE MEDICAL PROFESSION

items.subtopic: The Practice of Medicine | ENDURING VALUES OF THE MEDICAL PROFESSION
items.question: What remains at the heart of effective patient care despite scientific advancements?
options: A trusting relationship between physician and patient.
Access to sophisticated imaging techniques.
Integration of bioinformatics into practice.
The use of electronic medical records.
items.explanation: Despite scientific advances, a trusting relationship between the physician and patient is central to effective care.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · The Practice of Medicine | ENDURING VALUES OF THE MEDICAL PROFESSION

items.subtopic: THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE
items.question: Which field has seen rapid expansion due to advances in vaccine platform technology and cryo-electron microscopy?
options: Genomics
Immunology
Vaccinology
Cell biology
items.explanation: The passage states that advances in vaccine platform technology and cryo-electron microscopy have transformed vaccinology.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE

items.subtopic: THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE
items.question: What is essential for physicians to learn about artificial intelligence methods?
options: How to replace human decision-making
Their value and limitations
Only their technical aspects
How to use them without understanding
items.explanation: The passage emphasizes the need to understand the value and limitations of AI methods.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE

items.subtopic: THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE
items.question: What is crucial for a physician when faced with complex clinical problems?
options: Ordering as many tests as possible
Relying solely on laboratory technology
Identifying key elements in history and physical examination
Ignoring incidental findings
items.explanation: The passage highlights the importance of identifying crucial elements in a complex history and physical examination.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE

items.subtopic: THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE
items.question: What is necessary for effective clinical practice besides sophisticated laboratory technology?
options: Advanced imaging techniques
Machine learning algorithms
Intuition, experience, and judgment
Big data analytics
items.explanation: The passage states that skill in the most sophisticated applications alone does not make a good physician; intuition, experience, and judgment are also necessary.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE

items.question: Which of the following is emphasized as a key component in obtaining an adequate patient history?
options: Focusing solely on recent events
Allowing patients to narrate their story without frequent interruption
Conducting only a methodical review of systems
Avoiding expressions of empathy and interest
items.explanation: Patients should be allowed to tell their own story of the illness without frequent interruptions, which helps in obtaining an adequate history.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 1)

items.question: What aspect of patient interaction can provide valuable insight into patients' perception and reaction to symptoms?
options: The chronological order of symptom appearance
Patients' medical records
Inflections of voice, facial expression, gestures, and attitude
Only the physical examination findings
items.explanation: Listening to patients and noting their body language can provide valuable insight into their perception and reaction to symptoms.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 1)

items.question: What is the purpose of a thorough family history in patient evaluation?
options: To identify only genetic disorders
To focus on rare diseases exclusively
To reveal risk factors for common disorders and identify exposures
To avoid input from multiple relatives
items.explanation: A thorough family history can reveal risk factors for common disorders, such as coronary heart disease and hypertension.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 1)

items.question: What should be the approach towards patient visits in current health care settings to avoid withholding of information?
options: Rushing through the encounter
Ensuring confidentiality and not rushing the encounter
Focusing only on physical examination
Avoiding any expressions of empathy
items.explanation: It is important not to rush patient visits as it may lead patients to withhold relevant information.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 1)

items.question: Why should physicians not be deterred from performing a thorough physical examination despite the availability of highly sensitive diagnostic tests?
options: Physical examinations are more cost-effective than imaging techniques.
Important clinical findings may have escaped detection by diagnostic tests.
Diagnostic tests always provide results that alter patient management.
Imaging studies can replace all forms of physical diagnosis.
items.explanation: A thorough and thoughtful physical examination may reveal important clinical findings missed by diagnostic tests.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 2)

items.question: What is an essential consideration when using laboratory and imaging tests in diagnosis?
options: Tests should be ordered only if they are inexpensive.
Physicians must appreciate the limitations of diagnostic tests.
All test results must lead to a change in patient management.
Diagnostic tests eliminate the need for physical examination.
items.explanation: It is essential to appreciate the limitations of diagnostic tests, including their fallibility and the potential for error.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 2)

items.question: What should be considered when evaluating screening-test abnormalities?
options: All abnormalities must lead to an in-depth workup.
Abnormalities always indicate significant disease.
Occasional abnormalities may not connote significant disease.
Screening tests are unnecessary for common diseases.
items.explanation: Physicians must learn to evaluate occasional screening-test abnormalities that do not necessarily connote significant disease.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 2)

items.question: Why is it important for physicians to use imaging studies judiciously?
options: Imaging studies are always unnecessary.
Unexpected findings may lead to iatrogenic complications or irrelevant diagnoses.
All imaging tests provide results that alter management.
Physicians should order as many imaging studies as possible.
items.explanation: Investigation of an unexpected abnormal finding from imaging studies may lead to iatrogenic complications or the diagnosis of irrelevant problems.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following best describes a key challenge in implementing team-based care?
options: Ensuring all team members have identical training.
Balancing efficiency with direct communication among colleagues.
Limiting patient interaction to reduce complexity.
Avoiding the use of electronic devices for communication.
items.explanation: Effective communication is inevitably the most challenging aspect of implementing team-based care, requiring a balance between efficiency and direct communication.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 1)

items.question: What is an important role of hospitalists in modern medical practice?
options: Providing outpatient care exclusively.
Managing long-term chronic diseases at home.
Being responsible for the care of hospitalized patients.
Directing critical care units.
items.explanation: Hospitalists are board-certified internists who bear primary responsibility for the care of hospitalized patients and whose work is limited entirely to the hospital setting.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 1)

items.question: What has driven the transition of managing complex disorders in outpatient settings?
options: The need for longer hospital stays.
Reduction in available medical technologies.
Efforts to reduce costs and availability of new outpatient technologies.
Increased patient preference for inpatient care.
items.explanation: This transition has been driven by efforts to reduce costs and the availability of new outpatient technologies, such as imaging and minimally invasive procedures.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 1)

items.question: Why is it important for different providers to introduce themselves and explain their roles?
options: To reduce the number of medical errors.
To ensure patients receive care from only one provider.
Because roles and responsibilities can be confusing for patients.
To limit communication among team members.
items.explanation: The roles and responsibilities of various team members can be confusing for patients, so providers should introduce themselves and explain their role.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary responsibility of a patient's principal or primary physician in coordinating care?
options: To ensure that patients receive only basic medical advice.
To coordinate collaborations among health professionals and provide cohesive guidance through an illness.
To handle all aspects of patient care independently without involving specialists.
To focus solely on the scientific advances available to the patient.
items.explanation: The primary physician must coordinate collaborations among various healthcare providers and offer cohesive guidance throughout the patient's illness.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 2)

items.question: How can physicians help mitigate the stress experienced by patients during acute illnesses?
options: By avoiding any communication with the patient to reduce anxiety.
By providing information in a clear, calm, consistent, and reassuring manner.
By ensuring that patients are never left unattended at any time.
By minimizing the number of healthcare professionals interacting with the patient.
items.explanation: Physicians can reduce stress by providing information clearly, calmly, consistently, and reassuringly to the patient.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 2)

items.question: What is a key aspect of evidence-based medicine in clinical practice?
options: Relying solely on anecdotal experiences from recent patient encounters.
Making decisions based on rigorously designed clinical trials and objective comparisons.
Ignoring expert consensus when making medical decisions.
Focusing only on the natural history of disease without considering new evidence.
items.explanation: Evidence-based medicine involves making decisions based on data from well-designed clinical trials and objective test comparisons.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 2)

items.question: What does the concept of a 'medical home' incorporate?
options: A focus solely on primary care without involving subspecialty care.
Team-based primary care with subspecialty care in a cohesive environment.
Providing medical care exclusively in hospital settings.
Limiting patient interactions to only the primary care physician.
items.explanation: 'Medical home' incorporates team-based primary care along with subspecialty care, ensuring smooth transitions of care.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 2)

items.question: Which quantitative tool is used to determine the probability of an event based on prior knowledge?
options: Diagnostic tests
Bayes' theorem
Clinical-practice guidelines
Multivariate statistical models
items.explanation: Bayes' theorem is used for determining probabilities based on prior conditions.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 3)

items.question: What are the two key metrics used to ascertain the accuracy of a diagnostic test?
options: Sensitivity and specificity
Positive predictive value and negative predictive value
Prevalence and incidence
Relative risk and odds ratio
items.explanation: The sensitivity (true-positive rate) and specificity (true-negative rate) are used to determine the accuracy of a diagnostic test.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 3)

items.question: What is one of the main benefits of clinical-practice guidelines for patients?
options: They ensure personalized treatment plans.
They reduce the need for physician judgment.
They protect against substandard care.
They eliminate all uncertainties in diagnosis.
items.explanation: Clinical-practice guidelines help protect patients from receiving substandard care.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 3)

items.question: What is a limitation of clinical-practice guidelines mentioned in the passage?
options: They are always up-to-date.
They account for individual patient uniqueness.
They tend to oversimplify medical complexities.
They eliminate the need for evidence-based medicine.
items.explanation: Clinical-practice guidelines tend to oversimplify the complexities of medicine.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following is a reason why outcome measures such as mortality, length of hospital stay, or readmission rates are typically risk-adjusted?
options: To account for the complexity of clinical conditions and background illnesses.
Because they are not reliable indicators of treatment success.
To simplify data analysis in large health systems.
Due to lack of standardized definitions across different hospitals.
items.explanation: Outcome measures are risk-adjusted because patients often present with multiple complicating background illnesses, which can oversimplify the complexity of a clinical condition.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 4)

items.question: Which domains are included in assessing a patient's health status or quality of life?
options: Bodily comfort, capacity for physical activity, personal and professional function.
Only cognitive function and overall perception of health.
Financial stability and access to healthcare services.
Nutritional intake and sleep patterns.
items.explanation: Assessments include bodily comfort, capacity for physical activity, personal and professional function, sexual function, cognitive function, and overall perception of health.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 4)

items.question: What is one method used to reduce medication errors when electronic options are not available?
options: Using handwritten prescriptions with clear handwriting.
Implementing verbal orders only.
Eliminating misreading of handwriting and highlighting important drug interactions.
Relying solely on patient self-reporting.
items.explanation: Medication errors can be reduced by eliminating the misreading of handwriting or by systems that highlight important drug interactions when electronic options are not available.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 4)

items.question: Which system is used in many health systems to assess patient satisfaction and other qualitative features?
options: Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9).
Hospital Consumer Assessment of Healthcare Providers and Systems (HCAHPS) surveys.
Electronic Medical Records (EMRs).
Institute for Healthcare Improvement (IHI) checklists.
items.explanation: The HCAHPS surveys are used by many health systems to assess patient satisfaction, access to care, and communication with healthcare providers.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 4)

items.question: Which of the following is a potential disadvantage of Electronic Medical Records (EMRs) as mentioned in the passage?
options: They can create distance between the physician and patient.
They eliminate the need for face-to-face contact.
They reduce the time needed for data entry.
They ensure error-free documentation.
items.explanation: EMRs can create a barrier to personal interaction if not managed properly.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 5)

items.question: What is one of the reasons for physician dissatisfaction with EMRs as described in the passage?
options: EMRs provide too much narrative information.
They require significant time and effort to maintain.
They completely replace clinical decision-making.
They ensure seamless integration among providers.
items.explanation: The maintenance and utilization of EMRs demand considerable time and attention, contributing to dissatisfaction.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 5)

items.question: According to the passage, what is a risk associated with taking shortcuts in using EMRs?
options: Increased efficiency in documentation.
Improved patient-physician interaction.
Reduced need for clinical knowledge.
Increased risk of errors.
items.explanation: Shortcuts like 'cutting and pasting' can lead to inaccuracies, increasing the likelihood of errors.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 5)

items.question: Which quality is essential for a clinician according to Francis W. Peabody?
options: Interest in technology
Interest in humanity
Interest in medical procedures
Interest in clinical outcomes
items.explanation: Francis W. Peabody emphasized the importance of interest in humanity for clinicians.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 1)

items.question: What is a key aspect of informed consent as described in the passage?
options: Patients must sign consent forms without questions.
Physicians should ensure patients understand risks and benefits.
Physicians can assume patient understanding after one explanation.
Informed consent is not necessary for minor procedures.
items.explanation: Physicians are responsible for ensuring that the patient thoroughly understands the risks and benefits of a procedure.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 1)

items.question: What should physicians avoid in their interactions with patients?
options: Instilling confidence
Offering reassurance
Being arrogant or patronizing
Communicating clearly
items.explanation: Physicians must never come across as arrogant, patronizing, impatient, or hurried.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 1)

items.question: What is the ideal patient-physician relationship based on?
options: Thorough knowledge of the disease
Mutual trust and communication
Frequent hospital visits
Advanced medical technology
items.explanation: The ideal patient-physician relationship is based on thorough knowledge of the patient, mutual trust, and the ability to communicate.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a trend that makes medical care feel impersonal?
options: Efforts to reduce health care costs
Increasing reliance on technological advances and computerization
Growing number of managed-care programs
Decreasing use of telemedicine
items.explanation: The passage lists decreasing use of telemedicine as a trend that makes medical care feel impersonal, but it actually mentions the growth of telemedicine.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 2)

items.question: According to the Charter on Medical Professionalism mentioned in the passage, which principle is NOT included?
options: Primacy of patient welfare
Patient autonomy
Social justice
Financial profitability
items.explanation: The Charter on Medical Professionalism includes primacy of patient welfare, patient autonomy, and social justice, but not financial profitability.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 2)

items.question: What should a physician be prepared to provide when offering care to a dying patient?
options: Information to family members and deal with their grief
Financial assistance for medical bills
A detailed prognosis of life expectancy
Assurance that the disease will not progress
items.explanation: The physician should be prepared to provide information to family members and deal with their grief, as well as feelings of guilt or anger.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 2)

items.question: What is a major challenge for physicians in maintaining the humane aspects of medical care?
options: Lack of technological resources
Increasing reliance on telemedicine and virtual encounters
Excessive patient autonomy
Overemphasis on social justice
items.explanation: The passage highlights the increasing reliance on telemedicine and virtual encounters as a challenge in maintaining humane aspects of medical care.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary reason phenotypes are not determined solely by genes?
options: Genes alone determine phenotypes.
Phenotypes are influenced only by environmental factors.
Complex interactions among genes, gene products, and environmental factors determine phenotypes.
Gene expression profiles do not affect phenotypes.
items.explanation: Phenotypes are determined by the complex interactions among genes and gene products and by the interplay of genetic and environmental factors.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)

items.question: Which field studies alterations in chromatin, histone proteins, and DNA methylation that influence gene expression?
options: Proteomics
Metabolomics
Epigenetics
Microbiomics
items.explanation: Epigenetics is the study of alterations in chromatin and histone proteins and methylation of DNA sequences that influence gene expression.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)

items.question: What does proteomics focus on?
options: The study of environmental exposures.
The entire library of proteins made in a cell or organ and their relationship to disease.
The genomic study of environmental species.
The range of metabolites in cells or organs.
items.explanation: Proteomics is the study of the entire library of proteins made in a cell or organ and the complex relationship of these proteins to disease.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)

items.question: What does microbiomics study?
options: The genomic study of environmental species.
Alterations in chromatin and histone proteins.
The resident microbes in humans and their significance to health status.
The range of metabolites in cells or organs.
items.explanation: Microbiomics is the study of the resident microbes in humans and other mammals, which together compose the microbiome.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)

items.question: What is a critical role that physicians must play in relation to rapidly advancing technologies?
options: Ensure these technologies are applied judiciously to patient care.
Ignore the advancements and focus on traditional methods.
Only use new information for research purposes, not clinical practice.
Avoid continuous learning as it is unnecessary.
items.explanation: Physicians must ensure that powerful technologies are applied judiciously to patient care.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)

items.question: What is an important consideration for physicians when providing genetic testing?
options: Understand the value, limitations, and implications of test results.
Rely solely on patient self-assessment for interpreting results.
Avoid discussing potential outcomes with patients to prevent anxiety.
Use genetic tests without understanding their specific applications.
items.explanation: Physicians must understand the value, limitations, and implications of genetic test results.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)

items.question: What is a significant impact of globalization on medicine?
options: Diseases are confined to their regions of origin.
Global travel has no effect on disease spread.
Diseases can spread beyond local boundaries due to global travel.
Physicians should only focus on diseases within their own country.
items.explanation: Global travel allows diseases to spread beyond their regions of origin, affecting other areas.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)

items.question: How has the Internet transformed medical practice?
options: It has reduced access to information for physicians and patients.
It provides rapid access to a wide range of medical information.
It eliminates the need for face-to-face consultations entirely.
It ensures all online medical information is peer-reviewed.
items.explanation: The Internet has transformed medical practice by providing rapid access to a wide range of information.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary goal of the 'pay-for-performance' movement in healthcare?
options: To reduce the number of physicians practicing medicine
To link reimbursement to quality of care and improve health standards
To increase the use of traditional fee-for-service care
To eliminate the need for hospitalization
items.explanation: The goal is to link reimbursement to quality of care, thereby improving healthcare standards.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 3)

items.question: Which type of contracts have replaced traditional fee-for-service care in many parts of the United States?
options: Fee-for-performance contracts
Capitated managed care contracts
Government-funded healthcare contracts
Private insurance-only contracts
items.explanation: Managed (capitated) care contracts have replaced traditional fee-for-service care.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 3)

items.question: What is one of the responsibilities of physicians regarding new medical technologies?
options: To avoid using advanced technologies in practice
To help shape the debate on their appropriate uses and ethical implications
To ensure all patients receive gene therapy
To focus solely on traditional treatment methods
items.explanation: Physicians have a responsibility to help shape debates on new technologies' uses and ethics.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 3)

items.question: What has been implemented to reduce fatigue among physician-in-training?
options: Increasing the number of hours they can spend in hospitals
Introducing strict training parameters by ACGME
Eliminating the need for patient handoffs
Reducing the emphasis on lifelong learning
items.explanation: Strict training parameters have been introduced to reduce fatigue and enhance learning.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 3)

items.subtopic: The Practice of Medicine | ENDURING VALUES OF THE MEDICAL PROFESSION
items.statement: A key principle in medicine is that it aims primarily at developing new technologies.
items.explanation: Medicine's primary aim is to prevent disease, diagnose early, and provide effective treatment, not just technology development.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · The Practice of Medicine | ENDURING VALUES OF THE MEDICAL PROFESSION

items.subtopic: The Practice of Medicine | ENDURING VALUES OF THE MEDICAL PROFESSION
items.statement: A trusting relationship between physician and patient remains crucial for effective care.
items.explanation: Despite scientific advances, a trusting relationship is still central to effective patient care.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · The Practice of Medicine | ENDURING VALUES OF THE MEDICAL PROFESSION

items.subtopic: THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE
items.statement: Artificial intelligence is expanding more slowly than our understanding of its optimal use.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that AI is expanding rapidly, perhaps more so than our understanding of how to use it optimally.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE

items.subtopic: THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE
items.statement: Precision medicine relies solely on advanced laboratory technology.
items.explanation: The passage states that precision medicine also requires nuanced clinical pathophenotypes and is not based solely on laboratory technology.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE

items.statement: The physical examination should be conducted from head to toe only when the history suggests a specific organ or part of the body is diseased.
items.explanation: A new patient's physical examination must extend from head to toe in an objective search for abnormalities, regardless of the history.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 1)

items.statement: The detection of subtle physical signs like faint murmurs or small masses requires keen sensory perception and an alert mind.
items.explanation: Identifying subtle physical signs involves not only sensitive senses but also a mind that is alert to these findings.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 1)

items.statement: Physicians should always order a battery of tests for every patient.
items.explanation: Ordering unnecessary tests can lead to substantial costs and potential complications without altering management.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 2)

items.statement: A single abnormal laboratory finding in an otherwise well person should always prompt a comprehensive workup.
items.explanation: An in-depth workup after the report of an isolated laboratory abnormality in a person who is otherwise well can be wasteful and unproductive.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 2)

items.statement: Electronic devices such as medical records and apps are sufficient to ensure effective communication in team-based care.
items.explanation: While electronic devices can aid communication, it is important to balance efficiency with taking the necessary time to speak directly with colleagues.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 1)

items.statement: Hospitalists are responsible for providing care exclusively in the hospital setting.
items.explanation: Most hospitalists bear primary responsibility for the care of hospitalized patients and their work is limited entirely to the hospital setting.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 1)

items.statement: The primary care physician should make all decisions independently without consulting specialists.
items.explanation: Primary care physicians must ensure that decisions are made in consultation with specialists.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 2)

items.statement: The hospital environment can be a source of stress for patients due to unfamiliar surroundings and procedures.
items.explanation: Patients often find the hospital environment intimidating, which contributes to their stress.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 2)

items.statement: Precision medicine aims to provide generalized treatment recommendations for large populations.
items.explanation: Precision medicine focuses on tailoring treatments based on individual patient characteristics.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 3)

items.statement: Diagnostic tests are used to complement the history and physical examination in reducing uncertainty about a diagnosis.
items.explanation: Diagnostic tests help reduce uncertainty by providing additional information beyond clinical evaluation.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 3)

items.statement: Social media is being used to share patient experiences with healthcare systems in real time.
items.explanation: Social media enriches the information available for medical decisions by assessing feedback and sharing patient experiences.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 4)

items.statement: Central-line infection rates have not changed significantly despite adherence to standardized protocols.
items.explanation: Central-line infection rates have been dramatically reduced at many centers by adhering to standardized protocols.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 4)

items.statement: EMRs completely replace the need for clinical decision-making by physicians.
items.explanation: Information technology is a tool and cannot substitute for physician-made clinical decisions.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 5)

items.statement: EMRs can disrupt the traditional narrative flow of patient information.
items.explanation: The structured format of EMRs can interrupt the continuity of the patient's medical history.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 5)

items.statement: Patients are considered as 'cases' or 'admissions' in clinical practice.
items.explanation: Physicians should view patients as individuals, not merely as cases or admissions.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 1)

items.statement: Transparency of information is essential when dealing with terminal illnesses.
items.explanation: Transparency in communication is crucial for patients facing incurable diseases.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 1)

items.statement: Physicians should focus primarily on the life-threatening disease rather than the individual patient.
items.explanation: The passage emphasizes that attention should be on the individual person, not just the life-threatening disease.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 2)

items.statement: Good communication among providers is essential for consistent information to patients.
items.explanation: The passage states that good communication among providers ensures consistent information is provided to patients.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 2)

items.statement: The human haploid genome has more genes than the microbes residing on and in the human body.
items.explanation: The human haploid genome has ~23,000 genes, whereas the microbes have more than 3–4 million genes.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)

items.statement: Epigenetic alterations are associated with a number of cancers and other diseases.
items.explanation: Epigenetic alterations are indeed associated with a number of cancers and other diseases.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)

items.statement: Physicians should ignore the implications of global travel on disease spread.
items.explanation: Physicians must be aware of how global travel can affect disease spread.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)

items.statement: The Internet has had a universally positive effect on the practice of medicine.
items.explanation: While generally positive, the Internet also poses risks due to misinformation.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)

items.statement: The Flexner Report emphasized the importance of acquiring clinical skills in medical education.
items.explanation: The Flexner Report transformed medical education, emphasizing scientific foundations and clinical skills.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 3)

items.statement: Physicians are no longer required to engage in continuing education or certification.
items.explanation: Physicians must provide evidence of competence through continuing education and maintenance of certification.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 3)

items.subtopic: The Practice of Medicine | ENDURING VALUES OF THE MEDICAL PROFESSION
items.assertion: A physician's role involves technical skill, scientific knowledge, and human understanding.
items.reason: These qualities are necessary because the patient is not just a collection of symptoms but a human being with emotions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why these skills and understandings are essential.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · The Practice of Medicine | ENDURING VALUES OF THE MEDICAL PROFESSION

items.subtopic: THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE
items.assertion: Machine learning and artificial intelligence enhance clinical predictive accuracy.
items.reason: They enable unbiased analyses from large data sets.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE

items.subtopic: THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE
items.assertion: The art of medicine is becoming less important with advancements in technology.
items.reason: Technology alone can solve all clinical problems.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the passage emphasizes that the art of medicine remains essential despite technological advances.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE SCIENCE AND ART OF MEDICINE

items.assertion: An experienced clinician can usually formulate a relevant differential diagnosis from the history alone.
items.reason: The physical examination and diagnostic tests are used to narrow down or reveal unexpected findings that lead to more focused inquiry.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 1)

items.assertion: The social history can provide important insights into practical considerations for subsequent management.
items.reason: Social history identifies types of diseases that should be considered.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 1)

items.assertion: Physicians must weigh the expense involved in laboratory procedures against the value of the information these procedures are likely to provide.
items.reason: Diagnostic tests often gain an aura of certainty regardless of their fallibility and the potential for error.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 2)

items.assertion: The act of a hands-on examination fosters the patient-physician relationship.
items.reason: Physical examinations provide detailed anatomic information that can be pivotal in informing medical decision-making.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as it pertains to imaging studies rather than physical examinations.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · CLINICAL SKILLS (part 2)

items.assertion: The growth of team-based care has led to new specialties in medicine.
items.reason: Differentiation in levels of care within hospitals has resulted in the emergence of specialties such as emergency medicine and end-of-life care.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 1)

items.assertion: Telehealth tools have reduced the need for inpatient hospitalization.
items.reason: Telehealth enhances connectivity with patients, reducing visits to clinics or hospitals.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but telehealth enhancing connectivity does not directly explain the reduction of inpatient hospitalization.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 1)

items.assertion: The primary care physician's role includes explaining the involvement of specialists to reassure patients.
items.reason: This reassures patients that they are in the hands of physicians best trained for their current illness.
items.explanation: Explaining specialist roles helps reassure patients, making it a correct explanation.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 2)

items.assertion: Medical decision-making should be based on anecdotal experiences.
items.reason: Anecdotal experiences are often biased and not as reliable as evidence-based practices.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because medical decisions should rely on evidence rather than anecdotal experiences.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 2)

items.assertion: Precision medicine incorporates refined recommendations for individuals within subgroups of diagnoses.
items.reason: The pathobiology, prognosis, and treatment responses of these subgroups are better understood through genomic analysis or phenotyping.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 3)

items.assertion: Clinical-practice guidelines eliminate the need for physician judgment.
items.reason: Guidelines provide a framework for managing patients with particular diagnoses or symptoms.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because guidelines do not eliminate the need for physician judgment; they are meant to aid it.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 3)

items.assertion: Electronic Medical Records (EMRs) enhance health care quality and patient safety.
items.reason: They provide rapid access to relevant data, historical and clinical information, imaging studies, laboratory results, and medication records.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains how EMRs enhance healthcare quality and patient safety.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 4)

items.assertion: Patient falls can be minimized by using sedatives judiciously.
items.reason: Sedatives help patients remain calm, reducing the likelihood of falls.
items.explanation: The assertion is true; however, the reason is false. Patient falls are minimized through appropriate assistance with transitions rather than solely relying on sedative use.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENT CARE (part 4)

items.assertion: Empathy and compassion are essential features of a caring physician.
items.reason: The secret of the care of the patient is in caring for the patient.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why empathy and compassion are essential.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 1)

items.assertion: Physicians should avoid discussing risks and benefits with patients.
items.reason: Patients possess limited medical knowledge and must rely on their physicians for advice.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because physicians are responsible for discussing risks and benefits, while the reason is true.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 1)

items.assertion: Maintaining humanism in medical care is challenging due to various trends.
items.reason: Trends include efforts to reduce health care costs and increasing reliance on technology.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why maintaining humanism is challenging.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 2)

items.assertion: Physicians should be aware of their own emotional responses to patients.
items.reason: This awareness helps ensure that the patient's best interest remains the principal motivation for actions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why physicians need to monitor their reactions.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · THE PATIENT-PHYSICIAN RELATIONSHIP (part 2)

items.assertion: The study of metabolomics can potentially predict organ dysfunction.
items.reason: Metabolomics involves studying the range of metabolites in cells or organs and their alterations in disease states.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why metabolomics may allow prediction of organ dysfunction.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)

items.assertion: The variability in patient results challenges traditional reductionist approaches.
items.reason: Traditional reductionism is effective for managing large numbers of variables and identifying preclinical disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; traditional reductionism struggles with the complexity and variability in patient results.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)

items.assertion: The Internet serves as a valuable resource for medical information.
items.reason: It provides instant access to a wide range of current information and resources.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)

items.assertion: Physicians should avoid using the Internet for medical information.
items.reason: The Internet is a reliable source of peer-reviewed medical content.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because physicians are encouraged to use the Internet, but it must be done cautiously.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)

items.assertion: The rapid pace of technological advances in medicine necessitates ethical considerations.
items.reason: New technologies can modify inherited predispositions and prolong life span, raising ethical issues.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 3)

items.assertion: The introduction of strict training parameters has eliminated fatigue-related errors.
items.reason: These parameters were introduced to reduce fatigue and enhance learning for physician-in-training.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reduction in errors is not guaranteed by the introduction alone.
items.reference: The Practice of Medicine · EXPANDING FRONTIERS IN MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 3)
